text:
# Two-thirds of entrepreneurs want to stop within ten years, but succession is faltering

[Sector forecast](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/index.html)

[Cross-sector](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/alle-sectoren/index.html)

03/12/2025 • 14 minutes reading

[Gerarda Westerhuis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/gerarda-westerhuis/index.html)

[Mario Bersem](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/mario-bersem/index.html)

[David Bolscher](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/david-bolscher/index.html)

Melanie Murk

[Albert Jan Swart](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/albert-jan-swart/index.html)

Show all authors

A survey among 519 entrepreneurs, conducted by Ipsos I&O on behalf of ABN AMRO, shows that 68 percent of entrepreneurs want to continue managing their own company for a maximum of ten years. Of this group, 22 percent are not yet looking for a successor, while 20 percent are looking but have not yet found a successor. The fact that a significant proportion of entrepreneurs do not yet have a successor indicates a high risk of discontinuity and thus the loss of employment, capital and knowledge.

## Download the complete report

Read the forecasts for all sectors in the report: 'Two-thirds of entrepreneurs want to stop within ten years, but succession is faltering'.

- [Forecasts of all sectors (December 3, 2025)

  361 KB](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/sectorprognoses-abn-amro-bedrijfsopvolging-dec-2025.pdf)

## View the urgency of follow-up within 10 years in 2 minutes

[![

Your browser does not support HTML video. Here is a [link to download the video](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/abn-amro-sector-bedrijfstransference-v5-original.mp4)

](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/abn-amro-sector-bedrijfstransfer-v5-thumbnail.jpg)](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/abn-amro-sector-bedrijfstransfer-v5-720p.mp4)

The aging population makes preparation for succession all the more urgent. Figures from the Central Bureau of Statistics (CBS) show that almost 13 percent of all entrepreneurs in companies with at least two employees are now 65 years or older; a significant increase compared to more than 7 percent in 2010. In the coming years, a large number of entrepreneurs will want or have to transfer their business. Without a well-planned succession process, companies run the risk of discontinuity and thus loss of employment, capital and the disappearance of accumulated knowledge and experience. The risk of closures increases, especially in sectors where there is insufficient follow-up and where many companies are being sold due to an aging population, such as agriculture and retail.

The transfer process involves complex financial, legal and emotional issues. The Ipsos survey shows that entrepreneurs mainly struggle with letting go of control, determining a realistic value of the company and the complexity of the legal and tax framework.

## Follow-up and transfer

When considering succession, it is important to distinguish between succession in management and transfer of ownership. These two processes can take place simultaneously, but also one after the other. For example, an entrepreneur can transfer ownership of his company to his children, but not yet give up his managerial position. Or vice versa, the entrepreneur retains ownership and transfers management to a new director.

Particularly within family businesses, business transfer takes on an extra dimension. Family businesses are the heart of the Dutch economy: these 300,000 family businesses account for more than 30 percent of all jobs in the Netherlands and almost 30 percent of the added value of the (non-financial) business community, according to CBS data, which only looks at companies with two or more employees. At the same time, these companies are often vulnerable when a transfer takes place, because business operations are strongly intertwined with the entrepreneur and the family. Personal relationships, emotions and business interests are often intertwined.

CBS data show that family businesses are relatively common in the sectors Agriculture (47 percent of all agricultural companies), Hospitality (39 percent), Automobile trade and repair (30 percent), Wholesale (26 percent) and Retail (24 percent). They are least common in the Information and Communications (8 percent) and Specialist Business Services (7 percent) sectors.

metadata.source_title: Two-thirds of entrepreneurs want to stop within ten years, but succession is faltering

text:
CBS data show that family businesses are relatively common in the sectors Agriculture (47 percent of all agricultural companies), Hospitality (39 percent), Automobile trade and repair (30 percent), Wholesale (26 percent) and Retail (24 percent). They are least common in the Information and Communications (8 percent) and Specialist Business Services (7 percent) sectors.

Until recently, it was self-evident that a company would pass on to the next generation. Nowadays it is becoming increasingly common to transfer management and/or ownership (partially) to a third party, such as private equity or a strategic buyer, or to the existing management or an employee. For example, research by EY Consulting in 2024 shows that family businesses are increasingly opting for succession within the family less and less automatically. While in 2014 family members were still encouraged to take over the company, the research shows that companies are now open to external successors. This trend is driven by factors such as the changing ambitions of younger generations, the increasing complexity and capital intensity of companies, and the professionalization of business operations, according to the EY research. Research from 2024 by Nyenrode in collaboration with RSM concludes that 50 percent of family businesses still want to keep the company in the family; 16 percent are considering sales to strategic buyers and 5 percent to private equity.

## Are you thinking about selling your company?

It's a big step, but you're not alone. Read how we can help you with a successful sale.

[Transfer your company with confidence](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/speciaal-voor/corporate-banking/bedrijf-lezen/index.html)

## Planning for succession mainly within the family

Our survey shows that 68 percent of entrepreneurs want to continue managing their company for a maximum of ten years. Of this group, 22 percent have not yet started succession plans, while 20 percent are looking but have not yet found a successor. This underlines that follow-up often receives attention late, despite the complexity of the process.

It is striking that entrepreneurs who want to stop within five years are more actively concerned with succession than entrepreneurs who want to continue for another five to ten years. Only 8 percent of the first group has not yet started follow-up, compared to more than 37 percent in the second group. Yet the problem of uncertainty is significant: more than a quarter of entrepreneurs who want to quit within five years are looking but do not yet know who will be their successor. This applies to almost 14 percent of entrepreneurs with a longer horizon.

Entrepreneurs who want to stop within five years more often have concrete succession plans. Nearly 24 percent of them indicate that the intended successor is a family member. We see this especially in the agricultural and fishing sectors, where family businesses dominate. In addition, almost 12 percent have an employee in mind and more than 8 percent someone from outside the company. It is striking that more than 12 percent indicate that they want to close the company; These are relatively often smaller companies, especially in sectors where scale and continuity are more difficult to achieve.

## Age not the only motive

Age is an important personal motive for wanting to transfer a company. For example, 43 percent of entrepreneurs who have succession plans cite age as an important consideration for transferring the company. Of all entrepreneurs aged 65 or older in our survey, 60 percent want to stay on for a maximum of five years, while 24 percent want to continue for another five to ten years. In the age category 55 to 64 years, these percentages are 39 and 45 percent respectively. More time for personal life and other interests also scores high at 30 percent.

Money is also an important motive for transferring the company. More than 18 percent cite the proceeds to be received from the transfer as the reason. A good financial return is necessary, for example, to realize a pension provision, to develop new business initiatives or to provide the new generation with resources for other goals. When selling to private equity, the entrepreneur can (partially) cash out, while when transferring within the family, a lower price or later payment is often accepted to keep the company in family hands.

Young entrepreneurs often see an acquisition by an external party as an opportunity to start a new company with the released capital. This so-called 'serial entrepreneurship' is common in sectors with a high share of young entrepreneurs, such as e-commerce, software development and IT services. CBS data shows that the share of entrepreneurs under the age of 30 has increased from 9 percent in 2010 to 16 percent in 2024.

## Transmission within the family

metadata.source_title: Two-thirds of entrepreneurs want to stop within ten years, but succession is faltering

text:
Young entrepreneurs often see an acquisition by an external party as an opportunity to start a new company with the released capital. This so-called 'serial entrepreneurship' is common in sectors with a high share of young entrepreneurs, such as e-commerce, software development and IT services. CBS data shows that the share of entrepreneurs under the age of 30 has increased from 9 percent in 2010 to 16 percent in 2024.

## Transmission within the family

Of all entrepreneurs who want to stay for a maximum of ten years, almost a quarter want to keep the company within the family. This offers benefits such as continuity, employee retention and the continuation of corporate culture and values. The survey shows that ensuring the continuity of the company (37 percent), the future prospects of the company (24 percent) and employee retention (24 percent) are important aspects for entrepreneurs.

If a family member does not have sufficient resources to take over the company at once, a solution can be found in a combination of a subordinated loan from the seller and (re)financing from the company. The Business Succession Scheme (BOR) can also play a role. The BOR reduces the tax burden on transfers within the family. The tax scheme offers exemptions and deferment of payment for inheritance and gift tax and is intended to support the survival of companies in the event of transfer through donation or inheritance. Agricultural companies in the Ipsos survey in particular indicated that they make use of this.

## Transfer to third parties

Transfer to third parties is also increasingly being considered, such as when succession within the family is not feasible. Younger generations sometimes have different ambitions, insufficient resources or lack the right competencies. Differences in vision and values ​​can also play a role.

When a suitable successor is lacking, entrepreneurs opt for a management buyout, for example (4 percent of entrepreneurs who want to quit within ten years). The current managers take over the company. This provides stability and guarantees continuity, because the existing managers know the organization and market well. We see an example in the manufacturing industry, where experienced production managers take over a family business to retain knowledge and customer relationships. A comparable option is an employee buyout (6 percent), in which employees become owners. This increases involvement and minimizes disruption.

If no suitable successor is found internally, a management buy-in (6 percent of entrepreneurs who want to stay for a maximum of ten years) can offer a solution. Entrepreneurs then bring in experienced managers from outside to continue the business. The motive here is often modernization and innovation. For example, 11 percent of entrepreneurs in the survey who have succession plans say they want to make room for other ideas.

Strategic acquisitions are another chosen route (more than 11 percent of entrepreneurs want to manage the company for a maximum of ten years). The entrepreneur sells the company to a party in the same sector, for example a competitor or supplier. The main motive is to realize economies of scale and synergy, for example in sectors where margins are under pressure due to rising costs. In the catering industry, consolidation can lead to more efficient purchasing and more flexible deployment of staff. This offers a solution for catering entrepreneurs who are struggling with rising costs, tight margins and staff shortages. In the food sector we see that customers are becoming increasingly larger and cost leadership is the dominant strategy. To counterbalance this, suppliers are also consolidating.

Private equity mainly plays a role in companies that want to grow quickly or where owners only want to sell a part and where financing with debt capital alone is not sufficient to achieve sufficient growth or generate revenue (3 percent of all entrepreneurs who want to stop within ten years). These investors bring capital, expertise and a network, which is attractive for companies that want to expand internationally or enter the next phase of growth. A team of managers that buys a company can also engage with private equity to obtain financing.

Although only a small percent of entrepreneurs consider a steward ownership structure, this can be an interesting option for family businesses or mission-driven companies without a successor. With steward ownership, control is separated from ownership: stewards have control, but not ownership. Profit remains primarily available to the company and is reinvested or donated to support the mission. The motive is to safeguard core values ​​and mission, without focusing on profit maximization for shareholders.

## Letting go of the company

Nearly nine in ten entrepreneurs who are involved in business succession not only have difficulty finding a suitable successor, they also mention challenges such as letting go of control, determining business value and complexity. The succession process can therefore take longer and affect business activities.

More than a third of entrepreneurs with succession plans experience letting go of control as a challenge. A large part of their lives takes place at the company with which a strong social and emotional bond has been created. Entrepreneurs develop products, build relationships with customers and create a close-knit employee family.

metadata.source_title: Two-thirds of entrepreneurs want to stop within ten years, but succession is faltering

text:
More than a third of entrepreneurs with succession plans experience letting go of control as a challenge. A large part of their lives takes place at the company with which a strong social and emotional bond has been created. Entrepreneurs develop products, build relationships with customers and create a close-knit employee family.

Agricultural entrepreneurs interviewed in particular find it difficult to let go of control over their business: half of them mention this as one of the most important challenges. These entrepreneurs usually have a strong bond with their agricultural business, because they are usually family businesses and the business is usually also their place of residence.

It also happens in the industry and transport sectors that older entrepreneurs postpone the search for a successor or relinquishing control for too long. There is then a risk that the company will not adapt sufficiently to rapidly changing markets. It may be advisable to offer space to younger, talented managers in a timely manner. This gives them the opportunity to develop and can ensure that the company responds in a timely manner to changing market conditions. This can have a positive effect on productivity and the value of the company.

Entrepreneurs who find it difficult to let go of control can gradually distance themselves from their company. With a suitable successor, an entrepreneur can gradually transfer activities. This makes the entrepreneur less dependent on the company. With unlocked days, entrepreneurs can discover interests in other activities and leisure activities.

In addition to the emotional bond, entrepreneurs are afraid that their mission and established company values ​​will be lost. The entrepreneur and successor must be able to agree on each other's views and future plans. If an intended successor is further removed from the entrepreneur, it is difficult to let go of control. Then we must first work on building confidence in the successor or acquiring party. An alternative way to maintain company values ​​is the aforementioned steward ownership. The profit rights can then be transferred separately, while the voting rights will rest with, for example, a foundation that monitors the mission of the company.

## Start on time and phased sales

Other frequently mentioned challenges concern the practical implementation of transfer. Entrepreneurs find it difficult to determine business value, understand legal and tax matters and find suitable financing. The company value may be lower than an entrepreneur expected, while the entrepreneur's pension income depends on the sales proceeds.

It is important to carry out a valuation in a timely manner and to gauge interest. But even then the actual yield can be disappointing, for example due to unexpected worsened economic prospects or increased interest rates. A gradual transfer can dampen the effect of such influences. Among entrepreneurs who are working on succession, phased sales are the most popular at 21 percent. Such a construction also has the advantage that the transfer can proceed more smoothly. Managers or children who buy a company cannot always finance the entire acquisition sum at once. This becomes easier with a phased sale. A phased sale also gives confidence to staff, customers and suppliers, because the entrepreneur remains involved in the transition phase.

## Economic growth and interest rates

Macroeconomic conditions largely determine the feasibility and form of transfer. When the economy is growing, companies are more attractive with higher profits, while recessions usually lead to lower cash flows and lower valuations. Interest plays a crucial role in this. A higher interest rate increases the financing costs of transfer; When interest rates are low, acquisitions actually become more affordable. Because many transfers within both the family and to third parties are financed with debt, interest rate movements have a direct effect on the success of this process.

When succession is successful, productive capacity is maintained and entrepreneurs can bring innovation, digitalization and sustainability. On the other hand, if succession fails, jobs, knowledge and capital are lost and economic dynamism may decline.

## Conclusion

Business succession offers both opportunities and threats. On the one hand, it offers opportunities for continuity, strengthening management and innovation through new perspectives. On the other hand, a lack of preparation can lead to loss of value, disruption of business processes and loss of employment, capital and knowledge. Critical success factors are a timely succession plan, clear legal and fiscal frameworks, and the alignment of interests between owners, management and financiers. Without these preconditions, succession can pose a threat to business value, while a well-organized process can be a driver for sustainable growth and strategic renewal.

## Sector forecasts 2026-2027

ABN AMRO expects reasonably stable growth for the Dutch economy. At sector level, ABN AMRO expects the following volume growth:

Here is a brief explanation of the growth figures per sector. For a more detailed explanation of the trends and developments in the sector, we refer to the report below.

### Agricultural

In 2025, production in the agricultural sector was significantly higher compared to 2024. Weather conditions were more favorable and production increased sharply after an initial dip. In addition, we only expect the effect of stopper schemes in the coming years.

metadata.source_title: Two-thirds of entrepreneurs want to stop within ten years, but succession is faltering

text:
Here is a brief explanation of the growth figures per sector. For a more detailed explanation of the trends and developments in the sector, we refer to the report below.

### Agricultural

In 2025, production in the agricultural sector was significantly higher compared to 2024. Weather conditions were more favorable and production increased sharply after an initial dip. In addition, we only expect the effect of stopper schemes in the coming years.

[Read also: Europe focuses on young farmers to ensure food security](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/agrarisch/europa-zet-in-op-jonge-boeren-om-voedingveilig-te-borgen.html)

### Construction

After a decline in demand, the construction sector will again have large order portfolios, especially in 2023, but growth in 2025 remained limited due to increasing complexity of construction projects and personnel shortages. From 2026 onwards, more previously started projects are expected to be completed.

[Read also: Consolidation in the installation industry, but self-employed persons in general construction remain elusive](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/bouw/consolidatie-in-installatiebranche-maar-zzper-in-algemene-bouw-blijft-onrijpbaar.html)

### Food

The food sector experienced an increase in volume in 2025, driven by increased purchasing power at home and higher exports. The forecasts for 2026 and 2027 remain favorable. Dealing with unpredictable chains and staff shortages remain high on the agenda of food entrepreneurs.

[Read also: Food companies remain attractive for takeover](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/food/foodbedrijven-houden-aantrekkelijk-voor-overname.html)

### Care

The healthcare sector is growing by 3 percent this year and by 3.5 percent in 2026. The healthcare sector already showed a significant improvement in results last year thanks to postponed government cuts and improved employability of healthcare providers. ABN AMRO expects that healthcare providers will also achieve good returns in 2025 and 2026. Thanks to the government's ample budget, volumes are expected to grow by 3 percent this year and by 3.5 percent in 2026. A number of rates that providers receive are also rising faster than inflation. The forecast for 2027 is 2.5 percent; the government probably wants to slow rising healthcare expenditure from 2027 to find room for other expenditures, such as defense.

[Read also: Healthcare entrepreneurs wait and see with business succession](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/healthcare/zorgondernemers-afwachtend-met-bedrijfsopvolging.html)

### Industry

The industry faced headwinds in 2025 from the trade war unleashed by US President Trump, but will leave the biggest shock behind it in 2026. Previously postponed investment plans are now being continued, which is driving demand for machines. The industry can grow by 3 percent in both 2026 and 2027. However, energy-intensive industries such as the chemical industry are struggling due to high energy prices in Europe and Chinese competition.

[Read also: Capital-intensive industry requires a lot of attention to continuity](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/industrie/capital intensive-industrie-reist-veel-aandacht-voor-continuiteit.html)

### Leisure

Our 2026 estimates for the leisure sector are uncertain. It remains difficult to estimate the effect of the VAT increase. The decline in overnight stays is somewhat offset by other subsectors such as growth in the travel industry. Overall, we expect a leisure sector contraction of -3 percent by 2026, followed by a normalization of volume growth in 2027 (1 percent) when the market has adjusted.

[Read also: Tourism is growing, but VAT increase puts pressure on leisure](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/leisure/voorzichte-groei-Vrijetijdssector-on Zekerheid-blijft.html)

### Retail

text:
[Read also: Tourism is growing, but VAT increase puts pressure on leisure](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/leisure/voorzichte-groei-Vrijetijdssector-on Zekerheid-blijft.html)

### Retail

For 2026 and 2027, ABN AMRO expects consumption to grow again by 1 percent and 1.2 percent respectively. The recovery in domestic demand is supported by rising purchasing power and improved consumer confidence. Our sector estimates for broad retail – retail trade, wholesale non-food and car trade and repair – have been adjusted upwards from 0.5 to 1 percent volume growth for 2026, followed by 1.5 percent in 2027.

[Read also: Stiff competition forces retailers to consolidate](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/retail/stevige-conleidie-noodzaakt-retailers-tot-consolidatie.html)

### Technology, media and telecom

The technology, media and telecom sector rebounded in 2025 with a growth of 4.5 percent, driven by digitalization, cloud migrations and strong investments in artificial intelligence. Growth of 5 and 4 percent is expected for 2026 and 2027, respectively.

[Read also: Strong growth returns in Technology, Media and Telecom](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/technologie/sterke-groei-keert-achter-in-technologie-media-en-telecom.html)

### Transport & Logistics

The transportation sector was hampered in 2025 by the trade war, which plunged global trade into chaos. In 2026, the sector will leave the biggest shock behind it. International trade is expected to pick up, while domestic growth is also picking up, such as in the construction sector and retail. The sector is expected to grow by 2 percent in 2026 and by 1.5 percent in 2027.

[Also read: Increasing complexity forces the transport sector to increase scale](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/logistiek/toenemende-complexiteit-dwingt-transportsector-tot-scalegroei.html)

### Business services

Business services will grow by 1.5 percent in 2025, 1 percent in 2026 and 1.5 percent in 2027. The sector is staying the course in turbulent times, despite staff shortages, challenges surrounding business succession and changing laws and regulations.

[Read also: Business services stay the course in turbulent times](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/zakelijk-diensten/zakelijk-dienstverlening-houdt-koers-in-turbulente-tijden.html)
